validity_gate.justification: Every task notebook calls llm.prompt() over GPQA Diamond questions and scores the model's actual parsed responses (delegate/answer/confidence, declared MSV dims+routing, binary SDT judgments). Runs crashed early at ~7/80 due to an API NoneType error, but valid model scores were produced on completed items, so the evaluation genuinely exercises a model.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is precise: GPQA correct_answer letters with empirical difficulty, robust JSON parsing that iterates backwards over all objects with keyword/letter fallbacks (_parse_json_response, _parse_binary_judgment), and well-specified multi-level scoring rubrics encoded in code (confidence-weighted reward, routing alignment thresholds, type-2 SDT).

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation tightly isolates behavioral vs declared metacognition: Task1 measures whether the model acts on uncertainty by delegating on empirically hard items, Task2 makes declared self-report consequential via routing, Task6 uses paired neutral/affective framing of the same question to isolate emotional disruption — deliberate paired conditions matching the stated construct.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-documented modules with extensive docstrings, _safe_prompt graceful error handling, fallback parsing, per-item CSV logging, completion-rate weighting, and SDT computation without scipy; uses kbench @task decorators idiomatically (though loop-based rather than evaluate()).

4_sample_size.justification: Each task evaluates 80 GPQA Diamond questions (160 prompts for paired/binary tasks), well above the 20-item threshold, even though the recorded runs failed partway through.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple distinct dimensions per task: delegation reward + scientific Delegation AUC-ROC, declared probe combines parseability + cross-question differentiation + routing alignment, Task11 computes full type-2 SDT (d-hat, type2-AUC, d*, MC efficiency) — all justified in markdown with interpretation notes.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook headers articulate the exact capability (behavioral vs declarative metacognition / calibrated delegation), why it matters, and how it differs from prior work, citing the MSV framework and CIKM/TheWebConf references; gap (declared-vs-behavioral coherence) is explicit.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Each notebook documents dataset provenance (80 GPQA Diamond, difficulty from 10-model runs on Turing HPC), scoring rubrics with exact thresholds, activation formulas, cost in prompts, and experimental aims — comprehensive enough to reproduce.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes specific sub-capabilities with explicit hypotheses and experimental controls: empirical difficulty gradient drives delegation scoring, paired neutral/affective conditions, and SDT meta-sensitivity vs object-level sensitivity contrast — attributing performance to the targeted metacognitive mechanism.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items carry difficulty (continuous), diff_bin, and category labels (13 scientific domains); scoring is stratified by difficulty thresholds (easy<0.45 vs hard>=0.45) and routing alignment is reported per regime, enabling a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook's recorded run is on a single model (openai/gpt-oss-120b); no cross-model comparison is present in any notebook's outputs.

validity_gate.justification: Each notebook calls llm.prompt() per row via evaluate() over the ITTIA CSVs and scores the model's antonym output with is_correct() plus kbench assertions; outputs show real model responses being judged (e.g., deepseek-v3.2 'false' / 'little').

1_answer_correctness.justification: Deterministic ground truth (correct_antonym) verified with a normalized first-token match against a large ACCEPTED_VARIANTS synonym set, handling case/punctuation and multiple valid antonyms — robust verification logic.

2_construct_validity.justification: Stroop-style design directly operationalizes inhibitory/attentional control: task1a congruent (context supports antonym) vs task1b incongruent (context contradicts), with conflict_effect = congruent_acc - incongruent_acc, plus switching-cost (task2) and combined-stress (task3) controls isolating the claimed capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean modular code, idiomatic use of @task + evaluate() over a DataFrame, prompt builder, normalization helper, split-file fallback to filtering the master CSV; well-commented and robust to answer-format variation.

4_sample_size.justification: Each split has well over 20 items (task1a=150, task1b=150, task2 splits 100-183, task5=360), far exceeding the threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Each notebook computes only accuracy (boolean assertion pass rate); the congruency/switching deltas are described conceptually across notebooks but no second performance dimension (e.g., calibration/latency) is computed within a notebook, and no LLM-judge bias mitigation applies.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook headers name the capability (conflict suppression / attentional control) and describe the congruent/incongruent contrast and how the Stroop effect is computed, but do not connect to a broader research gap relative to existing benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebook markdown explains the trial type, pairing, and conflict-effect formula and the dataset splits, but lacks column-schema documentation, ground-truth construction provenance, and metric-interpretation detail across the full task page.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a specific sub-capability (prepotent-response inhibition) with explicit experimental controls: congruent baseline vs incongruent interference, conflict_strength tiers, and ceiling conditions, enabling attribution of performance deltas to inhibition.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a labeled taxonomy (congruent vs incongruent, conflict_strength low/.../high, non-switch vs post-switch, rule-A/rule-B/stress-ceiling) split into separate CSVs, enabling per-condition gradient analysis.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each individual notebook ran on a single model (eight on deepseek-v3.2, one on claude-sonnet-4-6); no notebook contains a multi-model comparison.

validity_gate.justification: subtle_framing_eval iterates the 100-row JSONL, sends each framing comparison to llm.prompt(), and asserts the model's normalized response against expected_label; the recorded run shows real model responses being scored.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth exists for all 100 items but many are inherently subjective (e.g., 'canine' vs 'doggy' which sounds more serious) with a single author label, and verification is exact-string equality after only lower()/strip() with no robustness for verbose or reasoning-prefixed outputs.

2_construct_validity.justification: Broadly targets framing/wording sensitivity, but the single-label-per-subjective-question design conflates the construct with agreement-to-author-preference and instruction-following (the model must emit exactly one of four strings), introducing moderate confounds.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and readable with column/label validation cells, but the eval loops row-by-row with assert_true rather than evaluate(), has no fallback parsing for non-conforming outputs, and minimal handling of malformed responses.

4_sample_size.justification: 100 evaluation items, well above the 20-item threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single correctness dimension is measured via pass/fail assertions; no second metric, no per-category breakdown computed, and no justification or bias discussion.

6_problem_statement.justification: The markdown names the capability (framing-of-statements / subtle-wording sensitivity) and gives a one-line motivation, but does not articulate why it matters or what gap it fills relative to existing social-cognition benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Partial documentation: the notebook prints columns/rows and validates labels, but there is no description of dataset provenance, how subjective ground-truth labels were derived, or how edge cases/ties are handled.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets the social-cognition/framing domain but does not isolate a specific mechanism; items mix perception shift, semantic equivalence, tone, risk framing etc. without a controlled manipulation or hypothesis, so it is unclear what success reveals.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into 10 explicitly labeled categories (10 each), and the category column is present in the data enabling per-category breakdown, though the notebook does not itself report per-category results.

10_model_coverage.justification: The recorded run shows a single model only; no cross-model comparison is present.

validity_gate.justification: mindgames_task.evaluate() runs each scenario's prompt through llm.prompt() and scores the model's parsed JSON against gold_answer_json; the run table shows real per-item model scores (deepseek-r1 results 0.00-1.00).

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is fully specified in gold_answer_json (agent_true_outcomes constrained to a closed VALID_OUTCOMES set, deceptive_turns) and verified deterministically with normalization of goal maps and turn sets, F1 for lie detection, and outcome-validity penalties — robust parsing with markdown-fenced JSON fallback.

2_construct_validity.justification: Implementation directly operationalizes strategic Theory of Mind: the model must infer each agent's hidden true outcome and identify deceptive turns in multi-agent negotiations, with scoring that combines goal inference, lie detection, and confidence calibration — closely matching the stated construct.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular helpers (_normalize_turns, _normalize_goal_map, _safe_confidence, _f1, penalties), idiomatic evaluate() over a DataFrame, glob-based dataset resolution, and the v2 notebook adds defensive parsing and invalid-outcome/lie-count penalties.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset has 1200 scenarios (v1 evaluates the full set; v2 filters to difficulty>=3, still hundreds of items), far above threshold.

5_metric_design.justification: The composite score integrates multiple distinct dimensions: goal-inference accuracy, deceptive-turn F1, and confidence calibration (calibration_term), plus invalid-outcome and lie-count penalties — more than one performance dimension with rationale in code structure.

6_problem_statement.justification: The task names strategic ToM / social cognition in multi-agent negotiation and the structured outputs make the target clear, but the notebook lacks a developed motivation connecting it to a broader research gap versus existing ToM benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The notebook is largely code with a clear schema implied by gold_answer_json and VALID_OUTCOMES, but there is little markdown documenting dataset provenance, the difficulty scale, or metric interpretation for reproduction.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (joint goal-inference + deception detection under negotiation) with a difficulty gradient (1-5) used as a control, and the v2 hard-subset isolates where models break down.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are stratified into five difficulty tiers (240 each) across varied settings (acquisitions, coalition formation, labor bargaining, cyber incident), and the difficulty column drives the v2 hard-subset filter, enabling per-tier gradient analysis.

10_model_coverage.justification: The recorded evaluate() run shows a single model (deepseek-ai/deepseek-r1-0528) across all rows; no cross-model comparison is present.

validity_gate.justification: Each notebook calls llm.prompt() with a gas-station dilemma and scores the model's free-text response via kbench.assertions.assess_response_with_judge against explicit criteria using judge_llm; a model is genuinely prompted and its response judged.

1_answer_correctness.justification: A judge rubric exists (3-5 criteria per notebook) but the 'correct' answer is contestable — the criteria force a single 'drive/move the car' conclusion while the model's reasonable answers (e.g., walk with a gas can, avoid stalling) would fail; criteria are pass/fail with no anchored levels, making correctness fragile.

2_construct_validity.justification: The task aims to measure heuristic/efficiency-paradox reasoning, but the prompt is ambiguous (an LLM can validly reason either way) so failures may reflect prompt ambiguity rather than the claimed capability; there is partial signal via the distance manipulation across notebooks.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and uses the judge API with a None-assessment guard, but it is minimal, hardcodes a single prompt per notebook, has no dataset/loop, and no description field on most tasks.

4_sample_size.justification: Each notebook evaluates exactly one hand-crafted prompt; even the 3-4 notebooks combined yield only a handful of items, far below the 10-item minimum, so a single judge call swings the score.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single pass/fail dimension (all judge criteria must pass) is computed, with no second metric and no mitigation of LLM-judge biases.

6_problem_statement.justification: The framing names a target (heuristic reasoning / efficiency paradox via the gas-station dilemma) but the notebooks themselves contain essentially no markdown motivation; the title carries the concept without explaining the gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The notebooks are code-only with no markdown, no documentation of the judge protocol, scoring interpretation, or why these specific criteria constitute ground truth.

8_capability_targeting.justification: There is an implicit manipulation (distance 5m / 50m / 5km across notebooks) targeting overgeneralization of the 'walk short distances' heuristic, but no controls within a notebook and no explicit hypothesis or quantitative delta is computed.

9_item_diversity.justification: The only variation is the distance parameter across three/four separate single-item notebooks; there is no structured taxonomy or difficulty stratification with per-condition reporting in code.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook's recorded run is on a single model with no cross-model comparison.
